coredata.dc:description: Bacterial infections pose a serious worldwide public health concern and play an important role in slowing or dramatically delaying wound healing. However, traditional antibiotics are faced with obstacles such as bacterial resistance and unsatisfactory biocompatibility, which has impeded further clinical translation. In recent years, antimicrobial nanomaterials have emerged as viable alternatives for combating bacterial infections, and carbon dots (CDs) have received particularly widespread attention due to their superior characteristics. In this work, a simple and eco-friendly one-step hydrothermal method was employed using the natural herbal medicine Eucommia ulmoides as a biomass carbon source to synthesize an Fe-doped CDs nanozyme (Fe-CDs) with good peroxidase-like (POD-like) activity, high biocompatibility, and strong antimicrobial activity for safe and effective antimicrobial therapy and the promotion of wound healing. L929 cells co-cultured with Fe-CDs did not show significant cytotoxicity and favored cell proliferation at appropriate concentrations. In addition, Fe-CDs catalyzed the decomposition of low-concentration H2O2 to ·OH, leading to enhanced antimicrobial activity. Both in vitro and in vivo experiments demonstrated that Fe-CDs exhibit potent antibacterial properties, the ability to promote cell migration and angiogenesis, and significant potential for promoting the healing of infected wounds. In summary, a green and safe antimicrobial nanozyme based on a biomass herbal medicine was developed in this work, offering promising insight into the development of novel antimicrobial materials and tissue regeneration engineering.

head.ce:title: Fe-doped herbal medicine-based carbon dots nanozyme as safe and effective antimicrobial and wound healing agent

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacterial infections pose a serious worldwide public health concern and play an important role in slowing or dramatically delaying wound healing. However, traditional antibiotics are faced with obstacles such as bacterial resistance and unsatisfactory biocompatibility, which has impeded further clinical translation. In recent years, antimicrobial nanomaterials have emerged as viable alternatives for combating bacterial infections, and carbon dots (CDs) have received particularly widespread attention due to their superior characteristics. In this work, a simple and eco-friendly one-step hydrothermal method was employed using the natural herbal medicine Eucommia ulmoides as a biomass carbon source to synthesize an Fe-doped CDs nanozyme (Fe-CDs) with good peroxidase-like (POD-like) activity, high biocompatibility, and strong antimicrobial activity for safe and effective antimicrobial therapy and the promotion of wound healing. L929 cells co-cultured with Fe-CDs did not show significant cytotoxicity and favored cell proliferation at appropriate concentrations. In addition, Fe-CDs catalyzed the decomposition of low-concentration H2O2 to ·OH, leading to enhanced antimicrobial activity. Both in vitro and in vivo experiments demonstrated that Fe-CDs exhibit potent antibacterial properties, the ability to promote cell migration and angiogenesis, and significant potential for promoting the healing of infected wounds. In summary, a green and safe antimicrobial nanozyme based on a biomass herbal medicine was developed in this work, offering promising insight into the development of novel antimicrobial materials and tissue regeneration engineering.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infections have turned into a major public health problem, and the development of bacterial antibiotic resistance as an emerging worldwide epidemic poses a major threat to human life and well-being [1]. If no action is taken to decrease bacterial resistance or make breakthrough antibiotic materials, bacterial infections are projected to kill 10 million people annually by 2050. Thus, the death toll of bacterial infections will surpass that of cancer, which currently kills the most people worldwide [2]. In light of this, the development of novel antimicrobial materials to supplement traditional antibiotic therapies will be crucial for ensuring global national health in the modern day.

Nanozymes are a kind of nanomaterial with enzyme-like catalytic properties, and their catalytic conditions and mechanisms are very similar to those of biological enzymes. Under certain physiological conditions, nanozymes can simulate biological enzymes, follow enzyme kinetics, and catalyze enzyme substrates to obtain products. Due to their simple preparation, low cost, high catalytic activity, good stability, low toxicity, and low drug resistance, nanozymes have been widely evaluated for application in biomedicine and analytical chemistry [3–5]. To date, >1200 different nanomaterials have been characterized as nanozymes, covering four classes of catalase: oxidoreductases, hydrolases, isomerases, and lyases [6]. Among the oxidoreductases, peroxidase (POD) catalyzes the conversion of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to highly reactive hydroxyl radicals (·OH), which play an important role in antimicrobial mechanisms due to their unique catalytic ability [7,8]. Based on the type of nanomaterial, nanozymes are broadly classified as metal-based [9], non-metal-based [10], metal-organic frameworks(MOFs) [11] and derivative-based nanozymes [12]. Among these nanozyme materials, non-metal carbon-based nanozymes have experienced a research boom the last few years because of their distinct structures and characteristics [13–15].

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional spherical carbon nanoparticles. Compared with other nanomaterials, CDs have the advantages of nanoscale size effects, good biocompatibility, good water solubility, easy surface functionalization, excellent photoluminescence, and easy synthesis. Consequently, CDs show excellent potential for application in several biomedical fields [16–19] (such as antimicrobial [20], anticancer [21], osteogenesis [22], biosensing [23], and bio-imaging [24] applications). In recent years, the safe and effective utilization of bioactive CDs in antimicrobial material systems has been reported, further confirming the strong potential of CDs for antimicrobial applications. Of the many carbon nanomaterials, CDs are the most closely related to their precursor raw materials [25]. Therefore, to prepare CDs that are biocompatible and retain effective antimicrobial activity, carbon precursor materials that already exhibit antimicrobial properties need to be selected. However, the majority of the raw materials that are currently utilized to prepare CDs include chemical reagents such as citric acid [26], glutaraldehyde [27], and so on. While CDs synthesized using chemical reagents have shown strong antimicrobial activity, cost and safety concerns have limited the further development of these CDs in the biomedical field. Because of the rising emphasis on the concepts of green chemistry, CDs prepared from natural biomass resources are becoming more and more common, such as glucose [28], leaves [29], Sapodilla fruits [30], milk [31], honey [32], and eggs [33]. Among these green precursors, herbal medicines are ideal as green carbon sources because of their high yield, low cost, abundant active ingredients, and ability to exert a variety of medicinal effects without generating a drug load [34,35]. Eucommia ulmoides is a traditional Chinese herbal medicine. Scholars at home and abroad have studied the active ingredients, pharmacology, toxicology, and applications of Eucommia ulmoides, reporting that this medicine can lower blood pressure, exhibits an antibacterial effect, has anti-tumor properties, can be used for diuresis, and so on [36]. The research of modern pharmacological have confirmed that the active chemical components of Eucommia ulmoides, including chlorogenic acid and myrcene coral glycosides, have strong inhibitory effects on Escherichia coli (E. coli), Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), and Streptococcus pneumoniae (S. pneumoniae), and accelerate wound repair [37]. In this work, the synthesis of CDs from biologically active Eucommia ulmoides was studied with the goals of matching or even exceeding the antimicrobial therapeutic effect of conventional antibiotics as well as promoting wound healing.

Another challenge that has hindered the further application of CDs is the poor electron transfer efficiency within their structure, which leads to limited catalytic efficiency [38]. Metal-doped CDs have gained a lot of attention recently as a new method of doping. Metal atoms are better electron donors than non-metal dopants because they have larger atomic radii and more unoccupied orbitals. The addition of metal ions changes the charge distribution and spin density of CDs and raises the amount of catalytically active sites, which enhances their catalytic performance [39,40]. Fe is an essential nutrient for the body and a potential target for skin care [41]. Because iron is widely available, highly biocompatible, and abundant, various Fe compounds have been utilized in biomedical applications [42,43]. Moreover, compared to other metal-doped carbon nanozymes, Fe-doped carbon nanozymes show stronger peroxidase-like (POD-like) activity [44,45]. Thus, adding Fe into biomass CDs is a desirable strategy for achieving enhanced POD-like activity to effectively enhance their antimicrobial properties.

Herein, a one-step hydrothermal method was used to synthesize CDs and Fe-doped CDs (Fe-CDs). The green biomass herbal medicine Eucommia ulmoides, which is safer than chemical reagents, was chosen as the carbon source to prepare these nanozyme materials. The obtained small-sized Fe-CDs have good biosafety, POD activity, and antimicrobial activity. Their successful preparation was demonstrated by a series of characterization techniques. As a biomass-based material, Fe-CDs show excellent biocompatibility in cytotoxicity tests and hemolysis tests. In vitro experiments demonstrated that Fe-CDs exhibit potent bactericidal effects on E. coli and display significant migratory and angiogenic capabilities in human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs). In vivo experiments demonstrated that Fe-CDs accelerate the healing of wounds infected with E. coli. This work offers a safe and efficient approach for the preparation of natural biomass-based CDs as nanozymes to fight bacterial infections and accelerates the healing of wounds.

Eucommia ulmoides extract was obtained from Sinuote Biotechnology Co. Ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O) and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were purchased from Aladdin Chemicals, Ltd. Methylene blue (MB) was purchased from Macklin Corporation. H2O2 (30%) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Dialysis bags (MWCO = 1000 Da) were purchased from MYM Biotechnology, Ltd. Cell Counting Kit 8 (CCK-8) was purchased from Solarbio. Calcein-Acetoxymethyl Ester (AM)/Propidium Iodide (PI) Double Staining Kit was purchased from Dalian Meilun Biotechnology Co. SYTO 9 Green Fluorescent Nucleic Acid Stain (SYTO-9) and PI were purchased from Roche Applied Science. Luria-Bertani (LB) and tryptone soy agar (TSA) were purchased from Beijing AOBOX Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Th reagents were used without additional purification.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-F200) was employed to analyze the morphology and structure of CDs and Fe-CDs. Ultraviolet-visible-near infrared (UV–vis-NIR, UV-8000) was utilized to measure the UV–vis spectra of CDs and Fe-CDs from 200 to 800 nm. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, FTIR-850) spectroscopy was employed within the range of 400 to 4000 cm−1 to verify the functional groups of CDs and Fe-CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo K-Alpha) was applied to confirm the composition and surface element states of CDs and Fe-CDs. The zeta potentials of CDs and Fe-CDs were examined using Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90.

To prepare the CDs, 2 g of Eucommia ulmoides powder and 40 mL of ultrapure water are placed in high-temperature Vacuum Drying Oven after ultrasonic shaking. Then, the hydrothermal reaction was performed at 200 °C for 10 h, then the reaction mixture solution was filtered with 0.22 μm filtration membrane. This CDs solution was dialyzed for 48 h. Finally, the CDs powder is obtained by lyophilizing the purified CDs solution in a vacuum lyophilizer.

Fe-CDs were prepared using a modified version of the same procedure. After hydrothermal synthesis and filtration, 0.5 g of FeCl3·6H2O was added to 50 mL of the filtered CDs solution. This solution was vigorously stirred at a speed of 500 rpm for 2 h. The stirred turbid solution was then re-filtered, dialyzed, and lyophilized to obtain Fe-CDs powder.

A CCK-8 assay was used to test the cell cytotoxicity and proliferation of Fe-CDs. L929 cells were cultured to 96-well plates (1.0 × 104 cells per well) and incubated in a thermostat (37 °C, 5% CO2). After 24 h, adding different concentrations of Fe-CDs (0, 50, 100, 200, 400, 800 μg·mL−1) and further incubated for 1, 3, and 5 days. On the day of the experiment, incubation was performed in the thermostat for 1 h by adding the CCK-8 solution. The absorbance at 450 nm was measured utilizing enzymatic standards and a microplate spectrophotometer. Cell activity was calculated according to [46]:

L929 cells were cultured into 24-well plates (2.0 × 104 cells per well) and used the same process as that employed for the cell viability and proliferation assay described in Section 2.4.1. The L929 cells were stained with AM/PI, and incubation was performed in the thermostat for incubated for 20 min. The L929 cells were observed using an OLYMPUSIX51 fluorescence microscope.

10 mL of rabbit whole blood was obtained by cardiac puncture. 0.5 mL of 2% potassium oxalate saline solution was added to the blood, and dilution was performed using 12.5 mL of saline (OD of 0.8 ± 0.3 for subsequent experiments). Different concentrations of Fe-CDs were dispersed in the saline as the experimental group, 0.9% saline was employed as the negative control group, and distilled water was used as the positive control group. Each solution was placed in a 37 °C water bath for 30 min. Then, 0.2 mL of diluted fresh anticoagulated rabbit blood was added, and each mixture was kept warm for 60 min. This was followed by high-speed centrifugation for 5 min. The absorbance at 545 nm was examined utilizing enzymatic standards and a microplate spectrophotometer. The hemolysis rate was calculated according to [47]:

Fe-CDs (200 μg·mL−1) were dissolved into HAc/NaAc buffer solution (0.1 М, pH = 6.5) containing TMB, and the reaction for 10 min. UV–vis spectroscopy was utilized to determine the change in the oxidized TMB (oxTMB) absorption peak intensity during this process to assess the POD-like activity of Fe-CDs. The control groups employed for this experiment were CDs solution (200 μg·mL−1) and HAc/NaAc buffer solution containing only TMB under the same conditions.

Fe-CDs and Methylene blue (MB) (5 μg·mL−1) were dissolved in HAc/NaAc buffer solution. The production of ·OH during MB degradation was determined by adding H2O2 (1 mM) and recording the change in absorbance of the mixed system at 665 nm using UV–vis.

Different doses of H2O2 (1.0 × 10−4, 1.0 × 10−3, 1.0 × 10−2, 1.0 × 10−1, and 0 M) were configured using HAc/NaAc buffer. Then E.coli (1.0 × 103 to 1.0 × 104 CFU·mL−1) was added in these different concentrations of H2O2 and incubated for 30 min (37 °C, 200 rpm). After incubation, 100 μL of each mixture was spread onto agar plates and placed in a constant-temperature incubator (37 °C, 18 h). The relative bacterial viability was assessed by the number of colonies. The antibacterial rate was calculated according to [44]:

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial activity of the Fe-CDs

ce:section.ce:para: A zone of inhibition (ZOI) experiment was performed to assess the antimicrobial properties of Fe-CDs against E. coli. The experiments were classified into four groups: Control (HAc/NaAc buffer solution), H2O2 (1 mM), Fe-CDs (200 μg·mL−1), and Fe-CDs + H2O2. 100 μL of E. coli (1.0 × 107 to 1.0 × 108 CFU·mL−1) was evenly spread onto agar plates. Next, four sets of filter paper sheets (6 mm) soaked in the four antimicrobial solutions were placed in the corresponding areas of the agar plates. Then these agar plates were inverted into a constant-temperature incubator (37 °C, 18 h).

The plate counting method was employed to evaluate the inhibitory properties of Fe-CDs against E. coli. Mixed systems consisting of E. coli (1.0 × 102 to 1.0 × 103 CFU·mL−1) and different treatments were incubated for 30 min (37 °C, 200 rpm). The final concentrations of Fe-CDs and H2O2 were 200 μg·mL−1 and 1 mM, respectively. Then, 100 μL of each mixture was spread onto agar plates and incubated in a constant-temperature incubator. The relative bacterial viability was calculated by the number of colonies.

Bacterial viability was detected by the fluorescent stain SYTO-9 and PI. Mixed systems consisting of E. coli (1.0 × 107 to 1.0 × 108 CFU·mL−1) and different treatments were incubated for 2 h (37 °C, 200 rpm). Each mixture was co-incubated with SYTO9 and PI for 30 min under dark conditions. Then, a drop of the co-incubated bacterial suspension was aspirated onto a slide, and the intensity of green and red fluorescence of each group was observed using CLSM. Green fluorescent means live bacteria and red fluorescent means dead bacteria.

Energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) was used to analyze the internal elemental distribution of the bacteria and detect the bacterial uptake of Fe-CDs. The experiment was classified into two groups: Control and Fe-CDs + H2O2. After treatment, the mixed E. coli (1.0 × 107 to 1.0 × 108 CFU·mL−1) and treatment systems were incubated for 2 h (37 °C, 200 rpm). Then each mixture was centrifuged for 5 min (6000 rpm), and fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde for 2 h. Then the procedure was followed by gradual dehydration with 50%, 70%, 90%, and 100% ethanol. Finally, the Fe content inside the bacteria was detected using EDS.

The morphological changes of the bacteria were observed using SEM and TEM after treatment and to visualize the bacterial damage. Two groups were used for this analysis: Control and Fe-CDs + H2O2. The experimental procedure was the same as that used to prepare samples for EDS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Migration and angiogenesis tests of Fe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of Fe-CDs on the migration of HUVECs was tested using migration and scratch assays. Control, H2O2, Fe-CDs, and Fe-CDs + H2O2 groups were employed for this experiment, and a transwell assay was applied to assess the migration of HUVECs in each experimental group. 5 × 103 HUVECs were placed in the upper chamber, and the complete medium was added to the lower chamber. After 24 h, the transwell chamber was removed, then 4% formaldehyde was used to fix cell and 0.1% crystal violet was used to stain the cells. The non-migrating cells were gently wiped with a swab. The cell migration was finally observed using fluorescence microscopy. In addition, a scratch assay was performed to evaluate the effects of Fe-CDs on the migration and repair abilities of HUVECs. Each well of the 6-well plate was inoculated with 1 × 105 HUVECs. After cell adhesion, lines were drawn at the bottom of the 6 plates with a marker and scratch lines were formed in each well using an enzyme-free pipette gun tip. After 24 h, fluorescence microscopy was used to observe the healed scratches of each group.

The tube formation capacity of HUVECs after different interventions was evaluated by using Matrigel. First, HUVECs were cultured to the third generation, and the HUVECs were treated with cell starvation. Next, Matrigel was injected to a 12-well plate (100 μL Matrigel per well) and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h. After the Matrigel dried into a gel, 1.5 × 105 cell suspensions were transferred to the 12-well plate and cultured at 37 °C for 6 h. The formation of tubular cell structures was observed using fluorescence microscopy.

All procedures involving the care and use of animals in this study were conducted in compliance with norms of Animal Welfare, and were approved by the Institutional Review Committee of the Stomatology Hospital of Shanxi Medical University.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo infected wound healing study, histology and immunohistochemistry
ce:section.ce:para:
A circular wound with a diameter of about 2 cm was created on each mice. Then, 10 μL of bacterial suspension (1.0 × 107 to 1.0 × 108 CFU·mL−1) was injected into the wound surface to establish an infected wound model. The mice were classified into four groups: (1) Control (HAc/NaAc buffer solution), (2) H2O2, (3) Fe-CDs, and (4) Fe-CDs + H2O2. The therapeutic interventions were given on days 1 and 3. Meanwhile, the traumas were also captured on 1, 3, 7, and 14 days, and the wound size was calculated according to: Size=
d 2
2
π . During the treatment process, exudates were collected from the infected wounds of each group on day 0 (12 h after the infection models were established) and day 4 to assess the bactericidal effect. On days 14, the mice are sacrificed and wound was collected. It is then fixed, embedded for hematoxylin-eosin staining (H&E), and immunohistochemistry staining (IHC).

The SD mice were sacrificed after 14 days, and five major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys) were collected and fixed overnight in 4% paraformaldehyde. Next, these samples were dehydrated, embedded, and sliced to prepare 4 μm thick tissue slices, which were then deparaffinized. All tissue sections were processed with H&E to detect the biosafety of Fe-CDs.
SPSS 23.0 software was utilized for the statistical evaluation, and statistically significant differences include *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001 and ns: no significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs and Fe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized by straightforward and eco-friendly one-step hydrothermal method using Eucommia ulmoides as a carbon source. Fe-CDs were also synthesized using Eucommia ulmoides as a carbon source and FeCl3·6H2O as the iron source (Fig. 1 ). The dose of FeCl3·6H2O was determined by observing the solubilities of samples prepared with different doses of FeCl3·6H2O and comparing their iron content via XPS (Table S1, Fig. S1).

The morphologies of CDs and Fe-CDs were examined by TEM. Fig. 2A showed that CDs was evenly distributed and had a uniform size. The diameters of the CDs ranged from 1 to 3 nm, and their average diameter was 2.92 ± 0.83 nm. In comparison, the doped Fe-CDs showed diameters ranging from 1.1 to 5.4 nm and had an regular diameter of 3.79 ± 1.09 nm. Thus, Fe doping led to slight enlargement compared to the CDs (Fig. 2B). The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs and Fe-CDs showed typical absorption peaks near 280 nm (Fig. 2C). These peaks can presumably be related to the n-π* transition within the CO and CC bonds of carbonyl functional groups and the carbon core, respectively [48]. In addition, the Fe-CDs solution showed an absorption peak at 207 nm ascribed to the electron transition of n orbitals [49]. The enhanced absorption intensity of Fe-CDs from the NIR region was ascribed to the charge transfer transition from metal to graphite. The functional groups of CDs and Fe-CDs were analyzed using FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2D). The absorption peaks of CDs and Fe-CDs located at 3409 cm−1 and 2933 cm−1 were linked to the stretching vibration of the O-H/N-H groups and the asymmetric stretching vibration of -CH3, respectively [50]. The absorption peaks at about 1618 cm−1 and 1408 cm−1 were associated to the stretching vibrations of the CO bond and -C-N/O, respectively [51]. Notably, the FT-IR spectrum of Fe-CDs displayed a new distinct peak at about 1131 cm−1, which was caused by the stretching vibrations of the metal-ligand bonds [52]. XPS was employed to examine the elemental composition and surface groups of CDs and Fe-CDs (Fig. 2E). The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of CDs showed peaks ascribed to C=C/C=N (283.9 eV), CC (284.8 eV), C-O/C-N/C-S (286.2 eV), and O-C=O (288.0 eV) (Fig. S2a) [53]. Meanwhile, the C 1 s spectrum of Fe-CDs also showed C=C/C=N, CC, C-O/C-N/C-S, and O-C=O peaks at 283.8 eV, 284.8 eV, 286.1 eV, and 288.1 eV, respectively (Fig. 2F). The high-resolution O 1 s spectrum of CDs showed peaks ascribed to CO (531.8 eV) and C-OH/C-O-C (533.0 eV) (Fig.S2b) [54]. The O 1 s spectrum of Fe-CDs also showed CO (531.7 eV) and C-OH/C-O-C (533.0 eV) peaks (Fig. 2G). In contrast to CDs, the high-resolution N 1 s spectrum of Fe-CDs showed a new peak at around 399.5 eV identified as Fe-Nx. (Fig. 2H, Fig. S2c) [55]. The high-resolution Fe 2p spectrum of Fe-CDs confirmed the presence of Fe (Fig. 2I, Fig. S2d). As points out in Fig. S3a-b, the Zeta potential for CDs was −15.4 mV, while Fe-CDs potential was −12.6 mV. Compared to CDs, Fe-CDs have a somewhat higher positive zeta potential., which was potentially related to the addition of Fe. Both materials had negative potentials, which was mostly related to the presence of surface functional groups such as hydroxyl groups and carboxyl groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity assay of Fe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The biosafety of the Fe-CDs nanozyme is a key concern for its clinical translation. Thus, the cytotoxicity of Fe-CDs on L929 cells was tested via CCK-8 assay. The experimental results showed that relatively better cell viability was achieved with a 200 μg·mL−1 dosage of Fe-CDs compared to other concentrations. However, the cell viability still remained over 85% when the concentration of Fe-CDs up to 800 μg·mL−1 (Fig. 3A). The results showed that Fe-CDs were well biocompatible and non-cytotoxic. Therefore, the Fe-CDs are highly biocompatible and non-cytotoxic. As shown in Fig. 3B, after exposure to different concentrations of Fe-CDs, the L929 cells showed a proliferative trend on days 3 and 5. In particular, the cell density significantly increased after exposure to 200 μg·mL−1 Fe-CDs. As shown in Fig. 3C, AM/PI live/dead Fluorescence staining also demonstrates that Fe-CDs did not show significant cytotoxicity and exhibited a notable proliferative effect, suggesting that they are highly biocompatible and appropriate for cell growth. L929 cell proliferation promotes the production of collagen fibers, facilitates fiber and capillary formation, and improves the appearance of granulation tissue during the early stages of healing of wounds.

The biocompatibility of the Fe-CDs nanozyme was assessed by hemolysis assay. Fig. 3D displays the experimental results of each group in a reaction with diluted anticoagulated rabbit blood followed by centrifugation. The positive control group's supernatant was red, demonstrating the destruction of the erythrocytes. In contrast, the supernatants of the experimental group and the negative group were nearly colorless, and hemolysis was not observed even when Fe-CDs was 800 μg·mL−1. These cytotoxicity and hemolysis tests confirmed the good biosafety of Fe-CDs, and the best performance was achieved at a concentration of 200 μg·mL−1. Therefore, this concentration was chosen for subsequent experiments.

Typically, the POD-like activity of nanozymes is evaluated via the catalytic oxidation of an artificial POD substrate in the presence of H2O2 to provide a variation in color. In the presence of POD-like nanozymes and H2O2, 3,3′,5,5′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) can be converted to blue oxidized TMB (oxTMB) (Fig. 4A) [56]. Thus, the POD-like activity of Fe-CDs and CDs was verified by TMB. As displayed in Fig. 4B-C, the control and CDs groups showed no absorbance and negligible absorbance. In comparison, the presence of Fe-CDs leads the color of TMB to significantly changed with time, and strong absorption was observed at 652 nm., Moreover, absorbance continuously increased with time, indicating the effectiveness of Fe-CDs for catalyzing the oxidation of TMB. Furthermore, the production of ·OH was confirmed using methylene blue (MB) and calculating the normalized absorbance of MB (A/A0). After adding Fe-CDs and H2O2, the characteristic absorption peak of MB at 665 nm gradually decreased with time (Fig. 4D-E). Those results show that Fe-CDs have strong catalytic activity and can be used as POD mimics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial activities of Fe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infection is a key factor that can slow or significantly delay wound healing. Preliminary experiments have demonstrated that Fe-CDs exhibit stronger antibacterial activity than CDs (Fig. S4). H2O2 has a crucial role in wound healing and is commonly used in clinical applications because of its effective antibacterial and antiseptic properties. Low concentrations of H2O2 promote the healing of infected wounds, while high concentrations of H2O2 tend to delay wound healing [57]. Evaluating the antimicrobial effect of different concentrations of H2O2 showed that at least 0.1 М of H2O2 was required to achieve excellent antimicrobial properties (Fig. S5). Therefore, by exploiting the POD-like catalytic activity of Fe-CDs (200 μg·mL−1), a low concentration of H2O2 (1 mM) can be converted to ·OH, resulting in safe and effective antimicrobial activity.

A ZOI experiment was performed to reflect the antibacterial Antibacterial activity of H2O2 and Fe-CDs [58]. The diameter of the inhibition circle in the Fe-CDs + H2O2 group was larger than that of the other experimental groups, indicating a stronger inhibitory effect (Fig. S6). Next, the inhibitory activity of Fe-CDs against E. coli was tested by plate counting assay. As shown in Fig. 5A-B, the H2O2 group and Fe-CDs group showed weak antimicrobial activity, while the Fe-CDs + H2O2 group achieved a high bactericidal rate of 94.82%. This can be ascribed to the generation of ·OH in the Fe-CDs + H2O2 group. The live and dead bacteria were stained by PI and SYTO9 fluorescent staining. Generally speaking, SYTO9 labels every bacterium, live or dead. But only bacteria with broken cell membranes are susceptible to PI's penetration. Therefore, dead cells can be identified by their red fluorescence using PI [59]. As illustrated in Fig. 5C, the control group exhibited apparent green fluorescence, and few cells with red fluorescence. This suggested that the majority of the bacteria were living. In contrast, nearly all of the bacteria displayed red fluorescence following PI staining, even though the Fe-CDs + H2O2 group also displayed green fluorescence following SYTO9 staining. Therefore, this group contained a relatively higher proportion of dead bacteria. Finally, confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) was used to observe the stained bacteria. This analysis confirmed the sterilization effect of Fe-CDs + H2O2. Interestingly, EDS analysis of the internal elemental distribution of the bacteria showed the presence of significant Fe content in the E. coli treated with Fe-CDs + H2O2. This strongly confirmed that Fe-CDs entered the interior of E. coli. To further pay attention to the antibacterial mechanism of Fe-CDs and to observe the degree of bacterial damage more directly, SEM and TEM were utilized to study the shape of the bacteria and the integrity of the bacterial membrane (Fig. 5E-F). The images of the control E. coli displayed smooth surfaces and undisrupted membrane structure. However, after Fe-CDs + H2O2 treatment, the Fe-CDs were attached to the E. coli cells. Furthermore, a significant number of clefts appeared and the E. coli surface started to wrinkle and become rough. Therefore, the bacterial cell membrane was clearly disrupted after treatment. Similar observations were made after TEM analysis. These results demonstrate that exposure to Fe-CDs + H2O2 disrupted the structure and increased the permeability of the E. coli cell membrane. This led to the leakage of bacterial contents, resulting in the death of the E. coli.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cell migration and angiogenesis of Fe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The formation of new blood vessels can enable the delivery of nutrients and oxygen to a wound, which speeds up wound healing. Therefore, a migration assay, scratch assay, and angiogenesis assay were carried out to evaluate cell migration and angiogenesis. After incubation for 24 h, the migration assay results showed that the Fe-CDs group and Fe-CDs + H2O2 group showed a higher number of cells that migrated to HUVECs, indicating a stronger migration force than the Control group (Fig. 6A). The scratch test results indicated that the scratch healing rate of the Fe-CDs + H2O2 group was almost 70.1%, which was much greater than that of the Control group (Fig. 6B, D). As illustrated in Fig. 6C, the Fe-CDs + H2O2 group presented a visible tubular structure, More junction numbers, and longer blood vessel length, indicating higher efficiency than the Control group(Fig. 6E-F). These results indicate that Fe-CDs + H2O2 can accelerate the migration rate of HUVECs and promote the formation of new blood vessels, indicating the good potential of utilizing this treatment to promote wound healing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo wound healing efficacy and cytotoxicity assay of Fe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The wound healing effect of Fe-CDs was assessed by establishing an E. coli infected wound model. As shown in Fig. 7A, wound healing usually occurs in a sequence of hemostasis, inflammation, proliferation, and remodeling [60].

The SD male mice were given four circular wounds on their backs, each with a diameter of roughly 2 cm, then 10 μL of bacterial suspension (1.0 × 107 to 1.0 × 108 CFU·mL−1) was dropped into the wounds to successfully establish the infected wound model. The SD mice were classified into four groups at random after 24 h: (1) Control, (2) H2O2, (3) Fe-CDs, and (4) Fe-CDs + H2O2, and the wounds were treated on day 0 and day 3. To assess the bactericidal effect of each group, exudate was collected from the infected wounds on day 0 and day 4. The cultured colonies showed that the Fe-CDs + H2O2 group had the most effective bactericidal capacity (Fig. S7). To observe the wound healing trend, pictures of the area of wound healing were obtained on days 1, 3, 7, 10, and 14 (Fig. 7B-C). Bacterial deposition and suppuration were found on days 7 in both the control and H2O2 groups. Notably, the Fe-CDs + H2O2 groups achieved almost complete healing after 14 days, while the other two groups still showed wounds of varying sizes. Fig. 7D shows that the most significant change between the four groups was observed on day 7.

On days 14, infected wounds were collected for observing the microscopic alterations by pathological analysis. Fig. 8A shows that the collagen fibers in the control and H2O2 groups were disorganized, and a large number of lymphocytes and neutrophils were observed (yellow arrows), indicating serious wound infection and relatively delayed repair. On the contrary, the skin tissue from the Fe-CDs + H2O2 group had a significant amount of connective tissue, there were many fibroblasts (green arrows), new capillaries (red arrows), and hair follicles (black arrows) obvious in addition to the collagen fibers' regular and orderly arrangement. Thus, the Fe-CDs + H2O2 group greatly promoted wound recovery. To study the inherent processes of wound healing in more detail, IHC staining were performed. The pro-inflammatory cytokine interleukin-6 (IL-6) was selected to assess the effectiveness of Fe-CDs in preventing infection Since the inflammatory induced by wound infection is one of the key factors of wound healing delay. In addition to preventing infection, factors related to wound repair are also important. Transforming growth factor-β (TGF-β) is a crucial cellular mediator in the healing of wounds because it stimulates cell proliferation and differentiation. Vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) plays a crucial function for the formation of new blood vessels. Therefore TGF-β and VEGF were also selected to determine the wound repair capacity of Fe-CDs. Higher levels of IL-6 expression were seen in the H2O2 and control groups, suggesting more serious infection. Conversely, the Fe-CDs + H2O2 group had the lowest IL-6 expression. The Fe-CDs + H2O2 group showed high levels of TGF-β and VEGF expression compared to the other groups, indicating a higher wound repair capacity (Fig. 8B). These experience results indicate that the Fe-CDs nanozyme is highly effective for the promotion of wound healing.

An in vitro cytotoxicity assay indicated the excellent biocompatibility of Fe-CDs + H2O2 (The final concentrations of Fe-CDs and H2O2 were 200 μg·mL−1 and 1 mM) with the L929 cells, which exhibited a certain proliferation effect (Fig. S8). Finally, the Fe-CDs + H2O2 system was evaluated for in vivo toxicity. on 14 days, the major organs of the SD mice (the heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys) were collected and carried out for H&E staining, and no significant inflammation or pathological damage was observed (Fig. 9 ).

In conclusion, Fe-doped biomass CDs with POD-like activity were synthesized by a simple and environmentally friendly one-step hydrothermal method for safe and efficient antimicrobial therapy and wound healing. The nano size of Fe-CDs facilitated their entry into bacteria for better antibacterial action. More importantly, both cytotoxicity and hemolysis tests showed that Fe-CDs have good biocompatibility, which is necessary for their clinical translation. An in vitro antimicrobial assay showed that Fe-CDs + H2O2 had a 94.82% inhibition rate. By adhering to the surface of E. coli, Fe-CDs disrupted the integrity of the cell membrane, which ultimately led to bacterial death. Fe-CDs + H2O2 exhibits significant potential in promoting the migration, repair, and angiogenesis of HUVECs, as demonstrated by migration and angiogenesis assays. Moreover, in vivo treatment showed that Fe-CDs + H2O2 contributed to fibroblast proliferation and collagen deposition, which accelerated infected wound healing. This work provides fresh insight into the development of novel antimicrobial materials and tissue regeneration engineering.
